root_cause: Need consistent error handling pattern for server actions that works with TypeScript
fix: Define action result type: type ActionResult<T> = { success: true; data: T } | { success: false; error: string; fieldErrors?: Record<string, string[]> }

Server action: 'use server'; export async function createPost(formData: FormData): Promise<ActionResult<Post>> { const parsed = PostSchema.safeParse(Object.fromEntries(formData)); if (!parsed.success) return { success: false, error: 'Validation failed', fieldErrors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors }; try { const post = await prisma.post.create({ data: parsed.data }); revalidatePath('/posts'); return { success: true, data: post }; } catch (e) { return { success: false, error: 'Failed to create post' } } }
Client: use useActionState (React 19) or useFormState for form state management

Display field errors: fieldErrors?.title?.map(err =>

{err}

)

root_cause: Need to invalidate related cache entries when data changes without over-invalidating
fix: Tag-based invalidation: store key-to-tag mapping in Redis sets
On write: await redis.sadd('tag:user:123', 'profile:123', 'posts:user:123', 'feed:latest'); await redis.set('profile:123', data, { ex: 3600 })
On invalidation: const keys = await redis.smembers('tag:user:123'); if (keys.length) await redis.del(...keys); await redis.del('tag:user:123')

Pattern-based: use key prefixes and scan (expensive, use sparingly): const keys = []; let cursor = '0'; do { const [next, found] = await redis.scan(cursor, { match: 'user:123:*', count: 100 }); cursor = next; keys.push(...found) } while (cursor !== '0')
Time-based: just set appropriate TTL and let data expire naturally (simplest approach)
Event-driven: emit event on write, invalidation handler subscribes and clears relevant caches

root_cause: Need comprehensive error monitoring and observability for production Next.js app
fix: Install Sentry: npx @sentry/wizard@latest -i nextjs
Configure: sentry.client.config.ts, sentry.server.config.ts, sentry.edge.config.ts
Wrap next.config.js: const { withSentryConfig } = require('@sentry/nextjs'); module.exports = withSentryConfig(nextConfig, sentryOptions)
Track server action errors: Sentry.captureException(error) in catch blocks
Use Vercel's built-in: Vercel Web Analytics + Speed Insights for core metrics
For Redis/DB monitoring: Upstash Console metrics, Neon Dashboard, Supabase Dashboard
Custom logging: use structured logging (pino) and forward to log drain

root_cause: Need components to respond to their container size rather than viewport size
fix: Enable plugin: npm install @tailwindcss/container-queries (or built-in in Tailwind v3.4+)
Mark container: className='@container'
Respond to container: className='@md:grid-cols-2 @lg:grid-cols-3'
Named containers: className='@container/sidebar' → child: '@md/sidebar:block'
Custom sizes in config: theme: { containers: { xs: '320px', sm: '480px' } }
Use for reusable components that might appear in different layout contexts (sidebar vs main)

root_cause: SQLite has limited ALTER TABLE support - cannot modify/drop columns directly
fix: SQLite only supports ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN and RENAME COLUMN
For schema changes requiring column drops/modifications, Drizzle uses table recreation strategy
Use drizzle-kit push:sqlite for development (destructive but fast)
For production migrations: drizzle-kit generate:sqlite creates proper migration with table recreation
Manual migration pattern: CREATE new table → INSERT INTO new SELECT FROM old → DROP old → RENAME new
Test migrations on local SQLite before running against Turso
Backup Turso before risky migrations: turso db dump your-db > backup.sql

symptoms: implement API response caching with Redis
root_cause: Need to cache external API responses to reduce costs, latency, and rate limit consumption
fix: Create cache wrapper: async function cachedFetch<T>(url: string, options: { ttl?: number; tags?: string[] } = {}): Promise<T> { const cacheKey = `api:${crypto.createHash('sha256').update(url).digest('hex')}`; const cached = await redis.get<T>(cacheKey); if (cached) return cached; const response = await fetch(url); const data = await response.json(); await redis.set(cacheKey, data, { ex: options.ttl ?? 300 }); if (options.tags) { for (const tag of options.tags) await redis.sadd(`tag:${tag}`, cacheKey) } return data as T; }
Usage: const weather = await cachedFetch<WeatherData>('https://api.weather.com/current', { ttl: 600, tags: ['weather'] })
Invalidate: async function invalidateTag(tag: string) { const keys = await redis.smembers(`tag:${tag}`); if (keys.length) await redis.del(...keys, `tag:${tag}`) }
Add stale-while-revalidate: store timestamp, serve stale while refreshing in background

root_cause: Need architecture that provides type safety from database schema to client components
fix: Layer 1 - Database: Prisma schema → generates TypeScript types, or Drizzle schema → inferred types
Layer 2 - Validation: Zod schemas share types with Prisma: const CreatePostSchema = z.object({...}); type CreatePost = z.infer<typeof CreatePostSchema>
Layer 3 - Server: Server Actions/API routes use Prisma types + Zod validation
Layer 4 - Client: Import shared types from lib/types.ts, use with React Hook Form + zodResolver
Layer 5 - Response: Define ActionResult<T> types for consistent server-client contract
Generate types from external sources: Supabase gen types, OpenAPI → zod codegen
Single source of truth: Prisma schema + Zod schemas = all types you need

symptoms: Supabase auth slow on first load
root_cause: Auth session validation adding latency to every request through middleware
fix: Cache auth state client-side: Supabase SDK handles this automatically with localStorage
Minimize middleware auth checks: only validate on protected routes, not static assets
Use lightweight session check in middleware: supabase.auth.getUser() (validates JWT without DB call on every request)
Preload session in root layout: const { data: { session } } = await supabase.auth.getSession()
Pass session to client via context to avoid waterfall: <SessionProvider session={session}>
For static pages: check auth client-side with redirect, not in middleware
